Play-offs

UEFA play-off Path A winner (Wales, Bosnia and Herzegovina, Italy or Northern Ireland)

UEFA play-off Path B winner (Ukraine, Sweden, Poland or Albania)

UEFA play-off Path C winner (Slovakia, Kosovo, Turkey or Romania)

UEFA play-off Path D winner (Czech Republic, Republic of Ireland, Denmark or North Macedonia)

IC play-off Pathway 1 winner (New Caledonia, Jamaica or DR Congo)

IC play-off Pathway 2 winner (Bolivia, Suriname or Iraq)

10 Japan have reached their eighth consecutive World Cup Credit: Getty



World Cup 2026 winner odds Via talkSPORT Bet, correct as of time of writing Spain - 4/1

England - 5/1

France - 7/1

Brazil - 8/1

Argentina - 8/1

Portugal - 12/1

Germany - 14/1

Netherlands - 20/1

Norway - 25/1

So, who are among the main contenders to win the 2026 World Cup?

Here, talkSPORT.com keeps an eye on England and Tuchel's chief rivals for the trophy via our power rankings.

The rankings are collated based on a team's outright winner odds, provided by talkSPORT Bet, for the 2026 World Cup.



5th - Argentina

talkSPORT BET Odds: 8/1

The reigning champions being so lowly may seem a surprise - until fans are reminded how rare it is for a country to go back-to-back.

Only Italy (1934/1938) and a Pele-inspired Brazil (1958-1962) have successfully defended the World Cup.

10 Winning the World Cup was the pinnacle of Messi's career Credit: Getty

Ironically, Argentina's triumph in Qatar denied 2018 winners France the opportunity to join the elite club.

La Albiceleste, themselves, also reached the final during their last World Cup as the holders but finished runners-up at Italy 1990.



Lionel Messi will now hope to do what Diego Maradona never could in what will be his last ever appearance in FIFA's elite tournament.

The eight-time Ballon d'Or winner has the added benefit of playing his club football at Inter Miami in the USA.

10

4th - Brazil

talkSPORT BET Odds: 8/1

The World Cup's most successful side can never be counted out - but Carlo Ancelotti has his work cut out to restore Selecao to the top.

The former Real Madrid manager is the first foreign boss to take charge of Brazil's national team in competitive games since 1925.



The Italian has overseen a mixed record of four wins, two draws and two defeats thus far as he gets to grips with the international game.

No country has ever won the World Cup with a foreign head coach, but Ancelotti's unrivalled CV gives Brazil as good a chance as any.

The 66-year-old has won the Champions League a record five times as a mana­ger, with knockout tournament football his speciality.

10 Ancelotti is one of the most decorated coaches in club football history Credit: Getty

3rd - France

talkSPORT BET Odds: 7/1

Les Bleus have reached the final in four of the last seven World Cups, and were a penalty shootout away from going back-to-back in Qatar.



Kylian Mbappe - who scored a hat-trick in that epic clash - is now France captain and at the peak of his powers with Real Madrid.

An all-star supporting cast of Michael Olise and Ousmane Dembele means Didier Deschamps has arguably the best team on paper.

However, it is yet to be seen what effect Deschamps' impending exit will have on the squad, having been in the post since 2012.

The Frenchman has already confirmed that he will leave after the World Cup, with defensive concerns also an issue for his side.

France conceded five goals against Spain in the Nations League in June, and were recently held to a 2-2 draw with Iceland.



10 France's attacking quality puts them among the favourites to win any major tournament Credit: Getty

2nd - England

talkSPORT BET Odds: 5/1

Like Brazil, the Three Lions have turned to a foreign manager with the clear and simple remit - win the World Cup.

If it's anything like qualifying, Tuchel will have no problem - with England guaranteeing a place in North America with utter ease.

Unfortunately, fans are all too familiar with the fact that it's the business end of tournaments where Harry Kane and co falter.

England head into the 2026 World Cup having reached two finals, one semi-final and a quarter-final in the last four major tournaments.



Such experience is one factor that fans have cause for optimism that a first major men's trophy since 1966 is finally within reach.

Another is Tuchel's record in knockout competitions, with the German winning over 70 per cent of such matches en route to guiding two teams to the Champions League final, lifting the trophy once with Chelsea in 2021.

10 England have been thriving under Tuchel's leadership Credit: Getty

1st - Spain

talkSPORT BET Odds: 4/1

The team that beat England in the Euro 2024 final currently hold the title of World Cup favourites to repeat their success from 2008-2010.

Even before downing the Three Lions, Spain had already beaten Italy, hosts Germany, and France en route to glory in Berlin.



The European champions then reached the final of the Nations League this year, albeit before losing on penalties to Portugal.

A scarier prospect for Spain's rivals is that so much of their recent success is based on young stars such as Pedri and Lamine Yamal, who will have only matured and developed further by next summer.

Luis de la Fuente also has an incredible squad depth at his disposal, with none of Yamal, Rodri, Nico Williams, David Raya, Marc Cucurella, Alejandro Balde, Gavi, Dani Carvajal, Dani Olmo, Marcos Llorente, or Fabián Ruiz even playing a single minute of the 4-0 win over Bulgaria in October through either injury or selection.

10 Morata captained Spain to glory at Euro 2024 Credit: Getty

World Cup 2026: How many teams, and how does qualification work?

The 2026 World Cup will be the first in FIFA history to see 48 teams be part of the tournament next summer.



Three of those are the hosts, Canada, Mexico and the USA, with a further 39 qualifying directly.

The final six spots will come from the respective play-off winners.

AFC: eight direct slots and one play-off slot

CAF: nine direct slots and one play-off slot

CONCACAF: three direct slots, three host slots and two play-off slots

CONMEBOL: six direct slots and one play-off slot

OFC: one direct slot and one play-off slot

UEFA: 12 direct slots and four play-off slots

10 There will be major changes from the World Cup in Qatar

World Cup 2026: Dates

The 2025/26 Premier League season will finish on Sunday, May 24, while the Champions League final will be held on Saturday, May 30.

The 2026 World Cup will then get underway on Thursday, June 11, and run until the final on Sunday, July 19.

FIFA is eager to keep the tournament's 'footprint' to 57 days, meaning there would be 16 days of preparation and then 39 days of competition.



There will be 12 groups of four, with the top two from each group plus the eight best third-placed teams progressing to the Round of 32.

There will be games every day from June 11 to July 7, with a rest day on July 8 prior to the quarter-finals.

June 24 to June 27 will see six games held each day.

The semi-finals will be held on July 14 and July 15, and there will be a bronze medal match on July 18, the day before the final.

News.title: French told to lay off croissants and baguettes to reduce risk of cancer
News.content:
OFF LE MENU French told to lay off croissants and baguettes to reduce risk of cancer

FRANCE has been told to cut down on national delicacies like croissants and baguettes due to a greater risk of developing cancer.

Food safety agency (ANSES) has recommended French people limit consumption of these much-loved products because of one potentially deadly ingredient.





The fertilisers used during the production process are said to contain high levels of cadmium.

This chemical element was classified as a carcinogen back in 2012.

Frequently used in industrial farming, it has been linked to a heightened risk of cancer.

More specifically, excessive consumption can lead to pancreatic, bladder, prostate and breast cancer, as well as osteoporosis, and cardiovascular and kidney damage.

Géraldine Carne, ANSES’ expertise coordinator, said: “If current exposure levels are maintained and no action is put in place, long-term adverse effects are likely to be more and more on the population.

“Limit the consumption of sweet and salty wheat products, such as breakfast cereals, cakes, biscuits.”

Researchers studied different possible sources of cadmium exposure, including food, water, air, dust, soil, cosmetics and smoking.

They discovered that diet accounted for up to 98 per cent of exposure in the non-smoking population.



Unsurprisingly, tobacco was the other major source of the chemical.

In French cuisine, some of the major cadmium sources are bread, pastries and cake, as well as breakfast cereals, pasta, rice and potatoes.

“The French levels are up to three or four times higher than those of other countries such as Belgium, England and Italy,” Carne told Franceinfo.

It is thought almost half the French population has been exposed to dangerous levels of the carcinogenic metal.

What is Cadmium? Cadmium is a naturally occurring heavy metal found in the Earth’s crust, often released into the environment through industrial processes and fertilisers.

It can be absorbed by crops grown in contaminated soil.

Staple ingredients like wheat can absorb cadmium, which is then used to make everyday foods such as baguettes and croissants.

Long-term exposure to high levels of cadmium can be harmful.

However, for most people, a balanced diet ensures cadmium intake remains well within safe limits.

Labelling the situation “worrying”, ANSES has urged the French government to drastically lower the maximum cadmium level permitted in phosphate fertilisers.



The current permitted level in France is 90mg/kg.

This is a far cry from various other European nations which have a lower limit of 60mg/kg.

To combat the growing risk, the public has been urged to introduce more legumes – such as chickpeas and lentils – into their diets.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

It has also been recommended that people consume a wide range of foods sourced from different regions.

This will help avoid repeated exposure to the harmful substance.

News.title: M65 traffic chaos as seven-vehicle crash sparks hour-long delays with drivers stuck in bumper-to-bumper queues
News.content:
TRAVEL TROUBLE M65 traffic chaos as seven-vehicle crash sparks hour-long delays with drivers stuck in bumper-to-bumper queues

A MAJOR UK motorway has ground to a halt following a multi-vehicle collision involving at least seven motors.

The M65 in Lancashire is currently closed westbound within Junction 3 – Brimmicroft Interchange – near Blackburn.



Emergency services have been scrambled to the site of the crash and are working to bring the chaotic scene under control.

Police, paramedics and fire crews have all responded to the ongoing incident.



Drivers are currently facing hour long delays and have been advised to plan their route accordingly.

A six mile long queue of traffic has built along the busy motorway in the wake of the pile up.

Police said they were alerted to the incident at around 4pm; it remains unclear if any serious injuries were reported.

National Highways said: “The M65 in Lancashire is CLOSED westbound within J3 (Brimmicroft Interchange near Blackburn) due to a collision involving at least 7 vehicles.



“All emergency services are on scene.

“Traffic is being diverted via the exit & entry slip roads. 60 min delay & 6 miles of congestion.”

News.title: Tragedy as boy, 4, and woman, 79,  die after four-car crash as cops appeal for dash cam footage
News.content:
SMASH HORROR Tragedy as boy, 4, and woman, 79, die after four-car crash as cops appeal for dash cam footage

A FOUR-year-old boy and a 79-year-old woman have died following a horror traffic collision.

Emergency services were scrambled to Grimblethorpe, Lincolnshire, on Sunday, March 15, after receiving reports of a multi-vehicle smash.



The four-vehicle crash involved a Blue Peugeot 108, a grey MG 1300, a white BMW X3, and a black Volvo XC60, cops said.

A fifth vehicle sustained collateral damage during the incident.



Following the fatal collision, paramedics rushed the 79-year-old to hospital but she tragically died a day later.

The young boy, who was a passenger in the Volvo, sadly passed away on Monday.

A probe to establish the circumstances leading up to the collision has been launched.

Officers are now appealing for anyone with any information or dash cam footage that may be relevant to come forward.



Lincolnshire Police said: “The woman, who was the driver of the grey MG 1300, was taken to hospital but died in hospital a day later.

“The little boy, who was a passenger in the black Volvo XC60, passed away on Monday 23 March.

“Investigators have carried out multiple lines of forensic inquiry, however Lead Investigator, Detective Sergeant Melissa Ablitt, is keen to understand more about the sequence of events that preceded the collision.

“She would like to speak with anyone who may have witnessed the collision or has CCTV, dash cam or other recorded footage that may have captured either of those vehicles prior to the collision itself.”

News.title: We sank £100ks into holiday homes in beach paradise but barely earn a PENNY… now we’re living off benefits and handouts
News.content:
IT was billed as the ultimate getaway dream – a chance to own a slice of paradise.

But Brits whose cash was sunk into properties in a top beach holiday destination today say they feel “conned” after barely seeing a penny back since 2019. And some have received nothing at all.



Instead they face mounting management bills for whole or fractional ownership of apartments and rooms in hotels in Cape Verde, the stunning archipelago nation off the coast of West Africa.



There are also fears over investments at a “ghost hotel” on Boa Vista that has no roofs or windows after work stalled before Covid.

Families are now planning legal action in an attempt to get compensation.

One pensioner told The Sun: “We were promised everything but it feels like we got next to nothing.”

As the island reels from a poisoning scandal linked to four deaths, we can reveal that investors have now employed a lawyer to take action against The Resort Group (TRG), run by an ex-business partner of Donald Trump.

Millionaire Rob Jarrett boasted of his involvement in the former Trump Tower and Hotel in Toronto after buying all the suites on four floors.



While Jarrett lives an opulent lifestyle, inviting pals to lavish parties on Cape Verde, ordinary Brits claim they have been left high and dry on investments.

Jarrett has built four hotels on the islands and its Melia Dunas Beach Resort & Spa on Sal opened with a celebrity fanfare in 2014 with star guests including Kerry Katona, model Danielle Lloyd, singer Tinie Tempah and Black Eyed Peas star Apl.de.Ap.

Up to 10,000 Brits are believed to have invested savings or pension cash into TRG, mainly through third party advisors.

They were lured by the promise of boosting their retirement cash at a time when David Cameron’s government was promoting ‘pensions freedom’ giving people more control over their benefit.

With properties already built on Cape Verde, TRG looked like a safe bet, but many UK investors claim they were never told it was actually a high-risk scheme more suitable for companies rather than individuals.



Although all investments can vary in returns, the investors say they expected the firm to keep its promise.

In September 2024, the Financial Conduct Authority, an independent watchdog, took action against four advisors and two partners at Essex-based firm St Martin’s Partners LLP for providing reckless pension transfer advice involving TRG.

The regulatory body fined Alec Cuthbert, Liam Martin, Adrian Douglas and Frank Oxberry a collective £590,544 for putting 547 customers at “significant risk” with an advice model that was “unlikely to be suitable for them”, including investments on Cape Verde.

Now those with properties on the islands have told how their futures have been “ruined”.

‘Future stolen’

Ex-civil servant Matthew Davis, 60, sank £110,000 from his pension into TRG in the hope of enjoying a comfortable retirement, travelling with wife Anita, 53, and helping his three grown-up children.



Instead, Matthew, of County Durham, is scraping by on disability benefits for mobility issues and alleges he’s not had a single penny from the firm since 2017 – two years after he first handed over his hard-earned cash.

He said: “Our lovely future has been stolen away from us.

“I invested at a time when David Cameron’s government was encouraging people to invest and heavily promoting its pension freedom reforms.



“I did all my due diligence. I took advice from a pension advisor and made sure there were no warning signs about TRG from the financial regulator.

“I was meant to get a return of up to £11,000 a year but I’ve had nothing for the past seven years.

“When I first invested I was told I was buying one luxury property for £159,000 but within a couple of months I got told they had a different scheme which would give me another one per cent a year.

“I now own two parts of two different properties on Cape Verde – but it means absolutely nothing.”



Place in the Sun dream

Retired farmer Selwyn Evans, 76, and wife Helen, 68, of Denbigh, North Wales, sank £50,000 pension money into an apartment at the Melia Tortuga Beach Resort in Sal in 2011.

They say they’ve had no income since May 2019 but were charged maintenance fees throughout the pandemic.

Helen said: “We’d seen Cape Verde on A Place in the Sun and were really attracted by the islands.

“We thought we would earn more from this investment than through our regular pension and buying an apartment would also give us, and relatives, the opportunity to have some great holidays there.

“The last payment we had was May 2019. We were still being charged fees throughout the pandemic.



We were promised so much yet delivered so little Ray Barnes

“I know they probably had to have security at the site but there was no breakdown of exactly what the funds were being spent on.

“People keep telling us Tortuga is really busy, yet we’re seeing no returns.”

Investors are also worried that work on TRG’s current project, White Sands on Boa Vista island, appears to have come to a standstill in 2019.

While the main buildings, which will house 835 suites, have been built, recent images reveal there are no roofs, windows or doors.

One 53-year-old woman, who asked not to be named, said: “I bought an off-plan at White Sands in 2015 but it’s never been built. There’s just no movement on it and everyone is asking why.



“I’ve had no returns and I’m really worried this is going to wreck my retirement.”

Ray Barnes was a self-employed financial consultant when he agreed to transfer £56,000 to buy part-ownership of an apartment at Dunas on Sal.

Ray’s pension advisor transferred his funds into a pension firm called Rowanmoor which acted as a ‘middle man’ between investors and TRG.

The firm went into administration in August 2022 following hundreds of complaints from clients that they were sold the wrong type of investments.

At the time the Financial Services Compensation Scheme said it had received almost 2,500 claims relating to TRG.



Ray, 72, of Warrington, and wife Barbara, had hoped the property would help top up their pension and that they could later sell their share.

Ray said: “My pension should be worth around £100,000 now but instead I’m living on state handouts of £14,000 a year.

“The hope was that we could sell the property when I reached retirement age but they are worth nothing now because nobody wants to buy them.

“We were promised so much yet delivered so little.”



Ex-nursery nurse Sandra Scarlett and husband Neil, both 64, bought on Dunas and say they have also had little cash but hoped their £310,000 villa could be widely used by family.

Yet when a relative turned up to use the property after the pandemic, he found it was being used as a storage room.

Sandra, of Bradford, Yorks, said: “We were in a fortunate position that we had just sold our house and could afford to buy the villa outright.

“So many people are in a worse position but the value has dropped to around £200,000, which is a huge amount of cash to lose.”

Families have now banded together to employ a lawyer to get compensation from TRG.



A spokesman for The Resort Group said: “We are very proud to have survived the devastation of the Covid 19 pandemic that saw the closure of our resorts for a period of over two years.

“We are now reaching recovery from the impact of the pandemic, which severely damaged the hospitality industry globally. 2025 was the first normalised year since Covid.

“All owners who are contractually entitled to payment of returns and who are compliant with their obligations are receiving payments and this will continue to improve.

“We cannot comment on advice that some clients may have received from independent third-party advisors but will willingly engage with any client who has any grievance or complaint”.

News.title: Teen girl, 17, dies of meningitis in Spanish holiday hotspot days before Easter getaways as UK outbreak ‘contained’
News.content:
DISEASE TRAGEDY Teen girl, 17, dies of meningitis in Spanish holiday hotspot days before Easter getaways as UK outbreak ‘contained’

A TEEN girl has died of meningitis in a Spanish holiday hotspot just days before Brit holidaymakers descend on the resort town for Easter getaways.

The 17-year-old died in Denia Hospital on the Costa Blanca with “symptoms compatible” with the deadly disease on Monday.





Her cause of death was confirmed yesterday, after a British outbreak of meningitis is said to have been contained.

The teen died in Denia, north of popular Benidorm – her nationality has not yet been released.

She reportedly lived in nearby Benissa.

After going to watch a basketball game in Valencia’s Roig Arena on Saturday, she began to feel unwell.



But the girl dismissed her symptoms as a cold, before she woke up the next day with spots covering her face.

She attended Benissa Health Centre but was rushed to Denia Hospital’s ICU by ambulance, where she died around midday on Monday.

The teen was described as an avid basketball fan who was “cheerful and affectionate” to all.

Public health teams traced the 17-year-old’s contacts, offering them preventative antibiotics to halt the spread of the deadly disease.



It comes after two other meningitis deaths in Spain – one in Extremadura and one in Andalucia, involving a teen and a five-year-old.

Concern has grown after the unprecedented outbreak in Britain killed two and infected 29.

But Spanish health authorities said there was no cause for concern, with current cases under quarantine.

It comes after a French worker died of meningitis in Normandy last week.

The employee worked at nuclear fuel company Orano – but the firm said there was no evidence that the case is linked to the UK outbreak.



Around 50 people had been in close contact with the infected employee.

They were locked down with a 14-day quarantine during which they cannot leave their homes.

Those who had contact with the employee were also offered a 48-hour course of preventive antibiotics.

News.title: I was falsely accused of rape aged 16 by woman who ruined my life… but I was just the tip of her sick iceberg
News.content:
SICK CON I was falsely accused of rape aged 16 by woman who ruined my life… but I was just the tip of her sick iceberg

A DAD-TO-BE who was falsely accused of rape as a young teenager has finally seen the serial liar brought to justice.

Kaylum Davis was only 16 when he was arrested on the street in front of his friends on suspicion of raping a girl he had never spoken to in person.





Stacey Sharples, now 31, was jailed earlier this month for perverting the course of justice after she falsely accused Kaylum and nine other men of raping her.

Kaylum’s life derailed while he was on bail for 18 months during the investigation. He was left unable to find work and he became the recipient of abuse in the street and online.

Four years after the charge was dropped the police approached Kaylum to support a case against Sharples but CPS didn’t take action.

Kaylum, now 28, then appealed the decision and finally Sharples was sentenced to four and a half years at Bolton Crown Court. She pleaded guilty to 10 counts of perverting the course of justice in relation to 10 separate men.



The then-teen started chatting to a 19-year-old Sharples on social media in December 2013. After exchanging a few messages they met on Boxing Day.

He told Manchester Evening News that he went to see a group of friends, both male and female, and Sharples was there.

Kaylum said he didn’t sit near Sharples or speak to her. She scrolled through her phone, not speaking to anyone, before getting up and leaving without saying goodbye.

Two days later Kaylum was arrested on suspicion of rape.



He was detained on the street while his friends watched and taken to a cell where he was held for hours.

During a police interview, Kaylum was asked explicit questions about orally raping Sharples, being violent towards her and even threatening her with a knife.

He said: “I was only 16, I was mortified at the use of explicit language and I was really scared. I tried saying I’d never been alone with her, I’d never spoken to her, but I felt they weren’t listening.”

Kaylum was posted bail for 18 months and struggled to find work while facing abuse in the street and online.

People would yell “Rapist” at him in the street or write horrible social media posts.



Kaylum described being constantly angry and feeling like his life was stuck.

The charges were suddenly dropped in 2015 without any explanation.

Officers reached out four years later to see if he would help with case against Sharples as he turned out to be the first in her string of allegations against multiple men.



Kaylum said he was happy to support a prosecution but then CPS said they wouldn’t press charges.

Finally after Kaylum appealed Sharples was charged and then jailed last month.

One victim told The Sun he met Sharples in 2019 on Badoo, where her profile had a topless picture.

He said: “Her messages said she was after one thing. She was in my house for about 20 minutes.

“We kissed then she said she didn’t want to kiss any more. I said no problem and got her a taxi home.



“Then she phoned and said I really hurt her.”

Sharples rang police and claimed the 40-year-old builder raped and attacked her.

Twelve cops arrested him at his Salford flat, he spent two days in cells and lost his job.



He was later told he faced no further action

Kaylum said: “We were all in court to see her jailed. It was the first time I’d seen her in 13 years – I would not even have recognised her and yet she had torn my life to shreds.”

Sharples got off lightly he told the paper as her lies ruined the lives of him and nine other men.

Kaylum said that genuine victims should be supported but making false allegations about innocent men was unforgivable.

He still sees comments on social media questioning whether he is innocent despite Sharples’ guilty plea.



“The stigma of a rape allegation never truly goes away. We had to live with the aftermath of her lies everyday” he continued.

Kaylum is looking forward to the birth of his first child later this year and is trying to move on with his new partner.

Detective Sergeant Steven Gilliland, who investigated this case, said, “No officer goes into a rape investigation with an intent to pursue the individual making that report, and we do not want genuine rape victims and survivors to think that is what will happen if they were to come forward.

“Genuinely, there is no better moment for a detective than when you secure a conviction for an offence such as rape, as it is amongst the most abhorrent of all offences and can cause lifelong trauma for those that experience it.

RECOMMENDED STORIES

“We took the allegations made by Stacey Sharples seriously, explored all lines of enquiry and swiftly made arrests or interviewed all of the men she accused.”



He continued: “Ultimately, as the evidence continued to demonstrate that the reports were untrue, coupled with the desire for justice from some of the men who had been falsely accused, it was right that we followed the evidence and pursued the individual who had actually committed a criminal offence.”

News.title: Moment drink driver FLEES 108mph crash that killed pal after downing pints and shots on five hour bender
News.content:
THIS is the moment a killer driver flees the scene after causing a fatal crash that killed his friend following a drinking binge.

Ross Neville, 35, boozed on beer and shots for more than five hours before he attempted to drive his pal home from town.





Cowardly Neville was sentenced to 11 years and 11 months behind bars after pleading guilty to charges including death by dangerous driving at Newcastle Crown Court.

Matthew Newton, 28, was sadly killed in the crash in November 2024 when his friend’s Ford Raptor careered off the road and collided with the metal crash barrier.

CCTV and dashcam footage from the night of the tragic crash reveals the reckless behaviour which ended up with Matthew pronounced dead at the scene.

Video from the Newcastle bar where the pair began their nightmare evening shows Neville sinking multiple pints and shots of spirit.

After hours spent drinking “to excess”, further footage from the car park and surrounding roads shows the duo enter the silver Ford Raptor truck – which was not insured.

CCTV shows Neville driving dangerously out of the city centre, speeding to overtake other cars.

Sickening dashcam footage from a lorry on the A69 near Throckley, Tyne and Wear, shows Neville whip past at a speed of 108mph.



The video was taken just moments before Neville drove the car off the road and into the metal crash barriers, killing Matthew.

Emergency services were called after the HGV driver came upon the mangled truck further up the road.

Footage shows the vehicle smashed to bits and debris flung across the dual carriageway.

When the lorry driver asked after the passenger, Neville lied and said his pal was fine.

In reality, Matthew was lying in the car with unsurvivable injuries, but Neville took the opportunity to leg it.



Further sickening CCTV footage captured the killer driver selfishly running away from the scene of the crash.

Police branded Neville “shameless” for fleeing the crash he had caused and leaving his mate lying there.

Sergeant Greg Huntley of Northumbria Police’s Serious Collision Investigation Unit, said: “This was a shocking incident – with Neville driving so dangerously and recklessly that he killed his friend Matthew.

“Worse still, he has then went on to tell the HGV driver who came across the crash that his friend was fine, before shamelessly fleeing the scene.

“It is clear to me that Neville was not a good friend – and he only had himself in mind that evening as he left Matthew with unsurvivable injuries in his car.



“Despite the challenges faced in the early stages of the investigation, we were able to trawl CCTV and financial data to piece together Neville’s actions.

“From this, we know that he consumed alcohol to the excess in Newcastle city centre before getting back in his pick-up truck to drive them home.

“He thought by running away he could hide how much he had drank – but thanks to tireless work by officers we proved this in other ways.”



Judge Edward Bindloss told the defendant: “You took no steps to help him. You didn’t even ring 999 for your friend. You did nothing for him.

“You were looking after yourself. You wanted to get away from the scene and perhaps wait for a time so that the alcohol that was in your blood had left your body.”

Mathew’s family, meanwhile, told of their heartache since losing their “beautiful” son and brother.

His mother, Anna, said: “November 28, 2024 will always be the date that broke my heart and changed my life forever. My life as I knew it ended that day to be replaced with the mere existence I live with now.

“I never thought I would ever be in a position to know what losing a child felt like.



“We have been robbed of Matthew’s future in the most traumatic, horrific way, with so many things stolen from us, seeing what he would have become, seeing him get married, having children of his own and watching him proudly as he fulfilled his dreams.”

Anna added the whole family was left trying to live with a “void” left in their lives.

“Life is hard now, a daily struggle filled with a constant overwhelming sadness, my heart aches every waking minute and the feeling of loss is painfully unbearable.”

In a poignant tribute, she remembered her son as “the most beautiful person”.

“[He] had the absolute best of friends, lived at home with us, would do anything for anyone, built up a successful business and was simply loved by everyone who knew him, he had the brightest smile and lit up every room he walked in to,” she said.



“At 28 years old he should have had his whole future ahead of him, now all I’m left with is an empty chair.”

Sgt Huntley added: “As ever, our thoughts firmly remain with all of Matthew’s loved ones as they continue to navigate a life without him.

“While no result will take away their anguish, I hope they feel a sense of comfort knowing Neville will spend a lengthy spell in prison.”

RECOMMENDED STORIES

Neville, of Canonbie, Dumfriesshire, admitted causing death by dangerous driving and driving without insurance.

As well as his 11 year sentence, he was also handed a seven-year driving ban, which will kick in upon his release from prison.

News.title: Powerful bodycam footage shows moment two cops save the life of choking baby as helpless mother stands by
News.content:
A BABY’S life was saved after police rushed to his home and rendered him aid.

The mother frantically handed the choking little boy over to cops as they jumped into action.





Maryland State Police shared the compelling body camera video on X and praised the officers for their work.

“I need help,” the mother can be heard screaming from the front yard as police run over.

“My baby is choking…he’s not breathing.”

The two officers rush to grab the boy, and immediately start patting him on the back while the baby was slouched over their knee.

“All he was doing was drinking…He’s just throwing up.”

Police continued to provide aid to the infant in the frenzied video.

“Come on buddy,” one of the officers says.

“Come on.”



After a high-intensity few seconds, the baby is handed over to the other officer when he realizes he’s breathing.

“Two @MDSP Troopers are being credited with saving the life of a choking infant in Southern Maryland this past weekend,” the department wrote in the post.

“Their quick actions and training made all the difference, preventing what could have been yet another tragic outcome.”

“Job well done by Maryland’s Finest!”

“Those guys are heros,” one user commented on the post.



“Way to go gentlemen,” another said.

On Valentines Day, another Maryland State Police officer saved a choking baby during a routine traffic stop.

“Without hesitation, Trooper First Class [Nicholas] Warren immediately called for emergency medical services personnel and removed the infant from the vehicle to perform life-saving first aid,” police said.

“In accordance with his training, he administered back blows for a few moments and removed the blockage. The baby then began breathing again.”

News.title: Sarah Ferguson charity suffers major blow amid Epstein scandal fallout
News.content:
Sarah Ferguson charity suffers major blow amid Epstein scandal fallout

Fergie had run Sarah’s Trust to help vulnerable communities around the world but last month it was disclosed that the good cause would cease its work "for the foreseeable future".

View 4 Images Sarah Ferguson with former Prince Andrew last year at the funeral of the Duchess of Kent (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

Sarah Ferguson ’s charity is set to be dissolved within weeks after it announced it would stop work for the foreseeable future due to revelations made in the release of the Epstein Files

The charity - Sarah’s Trust - was established in 2020 to support vulnerable communities and back education programmes around the world. But last month it was disclosed that the good cause would cease its work "for the foreseeable future" due to revelations over the former Prince Andrew.

The Charity Commission - which regulates charities in England and Wales - has now placed a “regulatory alert” on Sarah’s Trust. The alert advises: “The Commission intends to dissolve this CIO three months from the date of this notice unless cause is shown to the contrary."

Real reason Sarah Ferguson has remained so elusive despite Andrew scandal READ MORE :

View 4 Images Sarah Ferguson with the former Prince Andrew last year (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

The alert continues: "Representations must be made to the Commission within three months." Alerts are issued regarding emerging threats like cyber-attacks, new legal obligations or safeguarding failures.

Last year it was revealed that the charity had suffered a huge drop in donations. Income dwindled to just £31,000 in 2024, compared to almost £150,000 in the previous 12 months.

One trustee, leading London heart surgeon Professor Ramzi Khamis, stepped down just days after Ferguson was named in the first raft of Epstein emails.

Ferguson spoke about a “turbulent” year and how every child deserves the right to “thrive,” just weeks before being engulfed in the Epstein scandal.

View 4 Images Sarah Ferguson with former Prince Andrew (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

Seven charities dropped her as a patron or ambassador in September after an email from 2011 revealed that she called sex offender Epstein her "supreme friend" and seemed to apologise for her public criticism of him.

Just days earlier Sarah’s Trust had sent its annual report to the Charity Commission and she wrote: “It is with deep gratitude and renewed purpose that I share this year's annual report.

“The past year has brought both personal challenge and profound clarity. My own journey with cancer has strengthened my belief in the importance of early detection, compassionate care, and the life-saving research being carried out by dedicated medical charities.

View 4 Images Sarah Ferguson last year with Tim Lawrence (Image: Ian Vogler / Daily Mirror)

“It has deepened my resolve to stand beside those working to prevent, treat, and ultimately cure this disease-and to ensure that others facing similar battles are never alone.

“I am especially passionate about championing the potential of young people. Every young person deserves the chance to thrive, to discover their voice, and to grow into their fullest, most confident selves.

“To everyone who has stood with us, thank you. Together, we can continue to bring light where it is needed most.” She signed the report 'The Duchess of York'.

She has since lost her royal title, along with her ex-husband, the former Prince Andrew. Sarah’s Trust says of its aims: “Sarah has been committed to children’s and wellness causes with a focus on education.

"She has travelled the globe to see the projects first hand and has personally raised significant donations for causes that share her passion for change.”

Article continues below

The Mirror has approached both the charity and Sarah Ferguson's representative for a comment.

News.title: Eamonn Holmes tells real story of falling asleep on air and 'hatred' for Anthea Turner
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Eamonn Holmes tells real story of falling asleep on air and 'hatred' for Anthea Turner

Legendary TV presenter Eamonn Holmes on falling asleep on set, his outlook on life now he uses a wheelchair for chronic back pain... and his hatred of pop star Mariah Carey

View 7 Images Eamonn Holmes is a Northern Irish broadcaster and journalist. He co-presented the breakfast television show GMTV for ITV, before presenting Sunrise for Sky News, then ITV's This Morning. He is now on GB News (Image: Gareth Cattermole/Getty Images)

Eamonn Holmes has hit back at reports claiming he fell asleep while presenting his GB News breakfast show, saying he was examining faulty equipment. He said: “I was just looking down at my mic pack to see why it was not working, as we don't have floor managers or camera operators. Apparently I was sleeping? Whatever!”

‌



Father-of-four Eamonn also revealed how he had healed a hate-filled rift with Anthea Turner - his co-host on the GMTV breakfast show from 1994 to 1996. He said: “I am now best friends with Anthea Turner. There used to be real hatred between us and then I realised that Anthea was playing a part that she has been told to do. She has been very successful at it. I spoke to her tonight before I came here.”

‌



Bee Gees star's secret son says Lulu is WRONG to claim he fathered a lovechild READ MORE :

He is less forgiving of singer Mariah Carey, who he once refused to interview. He said: “I have interviewed her a number of times and she is always a complete pain in the a**. “ Recalling one particular interview, he said: “She comes in and lays down on a chaise lounge. I was told Miss Carey was very tired, as she had been up from 9am. I had been up from 4am. So I lay down beside her. Someone said ‘what are you doing?’ There were eruptions.

‌



7 View 7 Images Eamonn Holmes pictured earlier this year (Image: Grant Buchanan/Dave Benett/Getty) 7 View 7 Images Eamonn once refused to interview Mariah Carey (Image: Variety via Getty Images)

“I said ‘there is only one way this interview is happening. We either both lay down or we both sit up. I was told Miss Carey would not be sitting up and I said ‘well, Mr Holmes won't be doing the interview.’ I took off and left.” Eamonn, 66, who now uses a wheelchair due to chronic back issues, was speaking to TV presenter Hayley Palmer at a live audience event at London’s Hippodrome Casino.

He also spoke about his relationship with disgraced BBC newsreader Huw Edwards, who was given a six month jail sentence, suspended for two years, at Westminster Magistrates’ Court in September 2024 for possessing indecent images of children. Eamonn said: “Huw Edwards? I knew him very very well and was going to meet him the week after he was arrested. Did I suspect anything was wrong with him? No.”

‌



Experiencing the world differently as a wheelchair user, Eamonn wants to see better access for disabled people. He said: “If you are in a wheelchair you are either at the front of the cinema or the back. Why can't you be in the middle? The thing that makes me laugh is rooms that are classed as accessible in hotels. They are not at all. Everything is wrong, the bathrooms and the showers. I would love to advise hotel people as they are no use to anybody.”

7 View 7 Images Eamonn and ex wife Ruth Langsford. The couple split in 2024 (Image: WireImage) 7 View 7 Images Eamonn and Anthea Turner, his co-host on the GMTV breakfast show from 1994 to 1996 (Image: GMTV/FREE)

‌



But the TV presenter, who split from his wife of 14 years, Ruth Langsford, 66, who was his partner for 27 years, in May 2024, does not support the UK benefits system. Now in a relationship with counsellor Katie Alexander, 43, Eamonn, who lives in a penthouse apartment in Kingston, south west London, said: “I was very much brought up in a poor family. Do I remember benefits? No.

“Do I remember going to school with no breakfast? I don’t see why it is my responsibility to feed your kids at school. The government keeps saying they have 500 new places for breakfast at school, but why is that my problem? I don't see why it is. I speak to teachers who say some kids are not toilet trained or can't use knives and forks, but what kind of parents are these people? I present the news every day and it makes me very angry what we fight for in this country. I don’t think we, as a country, can be responsible for the whole world’s problems."

7 View 7 Images Eamonn Holmes and counsellor Katie Alexander, with whom he is now in a relationship (Image: w8media) 7 View 7 Images Eamonn denies falling asleep on TV (Image: GB News)

Article continues below

Eamonn is also critical of standards on TV. He said: “I see people on television and have no talent whatsoever. I don't find it funny and you see some useless person who can't read, write or get a joke out. It is incredible.” He also says meagre budgets mean TV standards are slipping, making programmes like ITV’s This Morning unaffordable. He explained: “It is a great pity. If you can't justify the audience then it's over. ITV will be the next one down the tubes. It will be sold to an American company and they will make it a streaming company.”

Of his GB News role, he added: “I am not proud of anything. I do my job. I enjoy what I do, but I don't sit and beat my chest about how brilliant it is.” Eamonn also criticised the state of UK politics. He said: “I think our political system in this country is screwed. I have got a lot of admiration for people who go into politics but the system corrupts them.”

News.title: Pupils 'secretly recording videos of teachers' as disturbing deep-fakes exposed
News.content:
Pupils 'secretly recording videos of teachers' as disturbing deep-fakes exposed

A recent trend on TikTok sees recordings of teachers being used and manipulated by AI-generated video to show them shouting abuse at kids, NASUWT teaching union warned

View 2 Images Pupils are secretly recording teachers and manipulating the footage with AI, a union said (Image: PA)

Pupils are secretly recording teachers and manipulating the footage with AI to make it look like they’re screaming abuse at kids, a leading teaching union has said.

‌



NASUWT union, which represents around 300,000 teachers across the UK, said the deep-fake clips are designed to damage staff professionally and personally.

‌



A recent trend on TikTok sees recordings of teachers being used and manipulated by AI-generated video to show them shouting and swearing at pupils in their classrooms, the union warned. It said the recordings are "leaving teachers exposed to false allegations, online abuse and significant mental distress".

‌



One video on the social media platform shows a teacher shouting and slamming down papers on the desk, with a caption saying: “Average UK school lesson.”

Another shows a male teacher screeching at students, telling them they will “spend the rest of your miserable lives in Leicester” because they weren’t working hard enough on their GCSEs.

Parents across UK to get new powers to limit teenagers' social media use under trial READ MORE :

View 2 Images 'Teachers are facing a terrifying new frontier of digital harassment' (Image: Getty Images)

‌



Matt Wrack, NASUWT’s general secretary, said: “Teachers are facing a terrifying new frontier of digital harassment. The ability to secretly record, edit or fabricate footage of staff poses a profound threat to their safety, dignity and professional standing."

He also referenced other examples of online abuse including a horrific case of upskirting - a criminal offence where someone takes a picture under a person's clothing - in which a teacher was forced to work with the same student again.

He said: “There are things that in any other walk of life would be deemed to be completely unacceptable, but in a school, are somehow deemed part of the job.”

‌



Mr Wrack said the same was true about increased physical violence against teachers, which he said is at a "shocking level".

The former General Secretary of the Fire Brigades Union and an ex-President of the TUC said: "I've dealt with violence at work issues before, but it strikes me that this is hugely under-recognised. There has been a major recognition of violence at work in relation to ambulance staff or retail staff, but not in relation to teaching."

Mr Wrack spoke to reporters ahead of NASUWT’s annual conference next week, where teaches are expected to voice their concerns over the Government’s special educational needs and disabilities (SEND) reforms. He indicated a storm was brewing and suggested thousands of teachers could take strike action amid fears of increased workload and pressure to implement the changes.

‌



"I think our frustration is that we don't feel that teachers' experiences have been adequately built into the SEND consultation document," he told reporters on Tuesday.

"There's quite a bit of frustration on our part on a number of fronts. Firstly, that the existing problems in schools, we don't think have been addressed. So issues around workload and hours have not been addressed. But we're now discussing a whole new raft of responsibilities.

"And then the detail of what is meant by inclusion and how it might be delivered. We've picked up on the £200million for training (teachers). While any increase in funding is welcome, you also have to measure that against what is actually needed. And we're not convinced that that figure delivers the scale of training that would be needed on this issue.

Article continues below

"And what we would not be willing to accept is teachers having some fairly basic training and then being told that they now have new responsibilities and they'll then be inspected against those new responsibilities."

News.title: BBC Traitors 2026 star reveals secret cancer battle saying he had 'no symptoms'
News.content:
BBC Traitors 2026 star reveals secret cancer battle saying he had 'no symptoms'

Traitors star Ben, who featured on Claudia Winkleman's hit BBC reality show earlier this year, has revealed his prostate cancer diagnosis in a social media post

View 3 Images Ben found fame on The Traitors earlier this year (Image: CREDIT LINE:BBC/Studio Lambert/Cody Burridge/Matt Burlem)

Traitors star Ben has opened up about his secret cancer battle. The reality star, 66, found fame on the latest series of Claudia Winklemnan's BBC series earlier this year. Now, in a candid post, Ben has taken to social media to reveal he was diagnosed with the "most common" form of the disease.

‌



Speaking in a TikTok video, he explained: "I was diagnosed with prostate cancer before I applied and was cast on The Traitors. Since then, prostate cancer has been confirmed as the most common cancer in the UK. Men are being diagnosed at an all-time high."

‌



Ensure our latest headlines always appear at the top of your Google Search by making us a Preferred Source. Click here to activate or add us as your Preferred Source in your Google search settings.

‌



Nine prostate cancer symptoms everyone should know after David Cameron's diagnosis READ MORE :

View 3 Images The reality star was diagnosed before he signed up for The Traitors

Ben also revealed that he had no symptoms prior to receiving the tragic news as he explained that he continues to have treatment. He said: "I never had any symptoms and people now tell me I am lucky that I was diagnosed. I tend to agree as I am under the NHS and they treat me every three months. I get tested to make sure my cancer is under control."

In his short video, Ben also noted that prostate cancer is the only form of the disease "full screening programme" in place, and called for this to change.

‌



A slew of his co-stars rushed to the comments section of his post to share their support, with Rachel Duffy, who won the programme alongside now-This Morning star Stephen Libby, writing: "Ben you are amazing."

Meanwhile, Roxy Wilson wrote: "Love to you Ben, great that you’re raising much needed awareness here." Fiona Hughes, who was the Secret Traitor on the show, said: "Sending you lots of cwtches Ben – so lovely of you to share your story and raising awareness."

View 3 Images Ben was eliminated from The Traitors fourth (Image: BBC)

‌



During his time on the series, Ben was a Faithful but got 'murdered' fairly early on in the programme, finishing 20th out of 24 players.

Last year, Prostate Cancer UK, released figures illustrated that the number of prostate cancer cases has surged ahead of breast cancer diagnoses during 2022 and 2023.

The data indicate a 25 percent rise in prostate cancer cases from 2019 to 2023, a spike attributed to increased awareness and more early detections.

Article continues below

NHS England's Clinical Director for Cancer, Professor Peter Johnson, praised the efforts, stating: "Thanks to the hard work of NHS staff and our effective awareness campaigns, we are diagnosing more men with prostate cancer at an earlier stage than ever before, so they have the best possible chance of receiving effective treatment.

"We know that coming forward for prostate checks can be daunting, but it could save your life, so we would encourage men to consult the prostate cancer symptoms advice available on NHS.uk, and to see their GP as soon as possible if they are concerned."

Like this story? For more of the latest showbiz news and gossip, follow Mirror Celebs on TikTok , Snapchat , Instagram , Twitter , Facebook , YouTube and Threads.

News.title: Snow buries UK as Met Office forecast reveals where more could fall in April
News.content:
Snow buries UK as Met Office forecast reveals where more could fall in April

Met Office weather forecasters says snow could impact some parts of the UK in April - surprise wintry showers fell today as temperatures plummeted

View 2 Images Snow could fall in April, the Met Office says (stock) (Image: PA)

Snow fell in some parts of the UK today - and now the Met Office says more could come next month.

‌



Today’s snow primarily impacted northern areas and hills, although hail fell in some places as far south as London. Forecasters have described this week’s chilly conditions as a ‘second winter’ descending on the country due to movements in the jet stream.

‌



Looking further ahead, the Met Office says we might not have seen the last of these chilly conditions as “wintry showers” and “colder interludes” are on the cards in April.

‌



The Met Office forecast for March 30 to April 8 suggests “high pressure to the southwest of the UK” will likely dominate our weather, with “weather fronts rounding the high to affect northern parts at times”. Wet and windy weather can be expected in the north as a result.

Content cannot be displayed without consent

UK weather maps turn icy blue as -3C Arctic freeze to bring snowfall this week READ MORE :

The Met Office says “drier and more settled conditions are more likely” in southern areas, while “high pressure is likely to spread across much of the UK”. This is likely to lead to more settled conditions developing widely.

‌



However, while temperatures are expected to be “near to or a little above average” in general, the Met Office adds: “Some colder interludes are possible, especially in the north, where there could be some wintry showers at times.”

BBC Weather also suggests there could be some snow over hills at the start of April. Its forecast for March 30 to April 5 states: “There is uncertainty surrounding next week's outlook. High pressure will try to build towards the UK at first but its exact position will have a large bearing on the resulting weather details.

View 2 Images Snow is expected to impact northern areas and hills (stock) (Image: Paul Campbell/PA Wire)

Article continues below

“Most likely we will see high pressure extending from the south-west, which should mean drier and milder conditions for at least the southern half of the UK. However, Atlantic systems skirting around the northern side of this high pressure could bring wetter and windier weather for a while across the north – particularly northern and western Scotland.

“At some point, the high pressure may recede or become positioned more to the west of the UK, allowing a cold front to bring rain farther south, although it will become lighter and patchy. This would also temporarily introduce chillier north-east to north-westerly flows with showers, mainly in windward areas, with wintriness over hills.

“However, even if that occurs then high pressure could soon move back across the UK, with more settled conditions resulting - but that would include chances of fog and light frost in places. An alternate scenario would bring high pressure across or east of the UK, with milder and drier weather overall.”

News.title: Truth about Huw Edwards' brain disease that helped paedophile avoid jail
News.content:
EXCLUSIVE: Truth about Huw Edwards' brain disease that helped paedophile avoid jail

WARNING: DISTRESSING CONTENT. At his sentencing for possessing indecent sexual images of children, the court heard how Huw Edwards was suffering from a degenerative disease that may have influenced his deviancy. Here, The Mirror explores the truth of those claims

Power: The Downfall of Huw Edwards trailer

Huw Edwards pleaded guilty to possessing sexual images of children, but hidden in the court documents were some alarming claims.

Once a highly respected broadcaster at the height of his profession, his downfall was perhaps one of the most unprecedented in the history of British journalism. And as sordid details about his shadowy private life were made public, the former BBC top earner brazenly claimed that medical factors contributed to his acts of depravity.

As was detailed in the new Channel 5 drama Power: The Downfall of Huw Edwards last night , the newsreader's life began to unravel in the summer of 2023, with a report in The Sun alleging that an unnamed BBC star had paid a young man for sexual pictures. Just a few days later, his wife, Vicky Flind, who has since filed for divorce, confirmed that the man was indeed her husband, who she clarified was "suffering from serious mental health issues" and receiving inpatient care. The Met Police investigated these accusations but found no evidence of crime.

From that point on, more allegations flooded in, with Edwards accused of inappropriately messaging a freelance journalist, and a sixth-form student, as well as fellow staff members at the BBC. Then, in November that year, things took a darker turn when the now 64-year-old was arrested in connection with a grim cache of indecent images of children shared on a WhatsApp chat. The images were sent to Edwards by convicted paedophile Alex Williams between the years 2018 and 2021, and included two videos of a boy believed to be between the ages of seven and nine years old.

View 2 Images Power: The Downfall of Huw Edwards will air this evening (Image: ©5 Broadcasting Limited / ©Wonderhood Studios / Photographer: Matt Towers / ©Wonderhood Studios Press Enquiries: press@channel5.com NOTE: Channel 5 images are for the use of Press outlets only. Any blogs requesting access to these images are required to seek approval from Channel 5 directly. Contact press@channel5.com While we will continue to send out the weekly PI booklet every Friday, we have now launched the ViacomCBS Media Hub. This is an all-encompassing press centre where you can find the latest PI, press releases and other relevant information. All Paramount pictures must not me altered or manipulated in anyway.)

After resigning from the BBC, in September 2024 Edwards was sentenced to a six-months in jail, suspended for two years, after pleading guilty to three charges of making seven Category A indecent images of children - the worst type - as well as 12 Category B, and 22 Category C images.

The sentencing report included submissions from Dr Appleyard, a forensic psychosexual therapist, and Michael Isaac, a consultant psychiatrist and neuropsychiatrist, who both made a series of alarming observations about the fallen star.

According to Dr Appleyard, Edwards was 'destabilised' when he joined social media and found he could chat directly with strangers. "His social media engagement presented as an easy way to manage his low mood and provided him with a number of men and women who were motivated to be sexual with him which not only boosted his fragile self esteem but allowed him to re-engage with his sexual interest in men which had been managed since 1994," the doctor said.

"The feelings of being desirable and unseen alongside Mr Edwards' unresolved sexual orientation created a perfect storm where he engaged in sexual infidelities." The court heard how his marriage had cooled as wife Vicky tirelessly cared for her dying mother, and Michael Isaac described him as "a complex individual" with 'volatile' moods.

He argued that Edwards' 'puritanical' upbringing with a father who 'behaved monstrously' within the family, along with depression and "low self-esteem, compounded by a sense of being inferior (by not getting into Oxford and going to Cardiff instead)," created a perfect storm for offending.

Suggesting that he was 'mentally impaired, Mr Isacc revealed that Edwards suffers from an "underlying cerebrovascular disorder" - small vessel disease (arteriosclerosis) - that would have influenced his decision-making and behaviour.

Describing it as a "cognitive disorder with behavioural disturbance", the expert said it "fed into his overall judgment and capacity to make decisions, mainly because of the development of his mood disorder, but compounded by the presence of the neurocognitive disorder, from 2018 and well established by the material time at the end of 2020.

"This condition will have reduced Mr Edwards' cognitive reserve, rendering him more vulnerable to the adverse cognitive effects of alcohol. Since approximately 2018, both of these conditions (neurocognitive disorder and mood disorder) are more likely than not to have impaired Mr Edwards' decision making and judgment, including his ability to comply with management instructions, reducing his normal inhibition and becoming 'stuck' in behaviours, such as obsessive messaging, whether appropriate or not, but at the same time detached from his communication, in that he would either forget what he had said or do soon a kind of 'autopilot,' without any real judgment of how such communications might be perceived or his actions impact on others."

View 2 Images In September 2024, Edwards was sentenced to a six-month jail sentence, suspended for two years (Image: BBC)

Senior District Judge Paul Goldspring accepted the reports as mitigating factors, commenting that, "his decision-making across the relevant time was or could have been adversely impaired by a mixture of mood disorder, neurocognitive disorder, and alcohol consumption."

As a result, he reduced the sentence from 12 months to nine months, with a further three months knocked off for the guilty plea. Explaning the decision to suspend his sentence, the judge said he had taken into consideration "strong personal mitigation, in particular your neurovulnerabilities at the time and your remorse, which I accept is genuine."

Now, a medical expert has weighed in on the extent to which arteriosclerosis, a degenerative neurocognitive disorder which results in the hardening and furring of the arteries, can influence risk-taking, poor judgment, and impulsivity.

Neurologist Dr Rab Nawaz Khan, M.D tells The Mirror that while it can alter thought processes, he does not believe that, in general terms, it leads to criminality. "Small vessel cerebrovascular disease can affect frontal-subcortical brain circuits that help regulate inhibition, judgment, emotional control, insight, and the ability to stop or reassess a behaviour," he said.

"In real life, that can show up as disinhibition, impulsivity, repetitive messaging, poor boundaries, reduced appreciation of consequences, and getting stuck in unhealthy patterns.

"That said, it is very important not to overstate what a neurological diagnosis can explain. A condition like arteriosclerosis or a neurocognitive disorder may lower the threshold for impulsive, reckless, or poorly judged behaviour, but it does not by itself create a specific pattern of sexual behaviour, a sexual preference, or a deliberate predatory motive. In other words, it may help explain loss of restraint or impaired self-control, but it should not be used as a simple one-to-one explanation for complex acts such as grooming or sexually exploitative conduct."

He concluded: "A cerebrovascular disorder, such as small vessel disease, can impair inhibition, judgment, and emotional control, so from a neurological standpoint, it can make a person more impulsive, more repetitive, and less able to appreciate consequences in real time. What it cannot do, on its own, is neatly explain a complex pattern of sexualised or exploitative behaviour. The fairest medical view is that it may have lowered behavioural brakes, but it should not be treated as a complete explanation for the conduct itself."

‌



In a recent statement, released ahead of Power: The Downfall of Huw Edwards being aired on Channel 5, Edwards once again put his own mental health at the fore, telling the Daily Mail: "I am making an effort to produce my own account of these terrible events. This is a slow process, given the fragile state of my health. I have been open about my struggle with persistent mental illness over a period of 25 years.

"What is less well known is the severity of that condition, which was managed successfully until the downward spiral which led to an appalling outcome. Mental illness is misunderstood by many, but can never be an excuse for criminality. It can, however, at least help explain why people sometimes behave in shocking and reprehensible ways, and why things fell apart for me in the way they did."

The statement is bound to infuriate former BBC co-workers, with a source previously claiming Edwards "betrayed the BBC by playing the mental health card" in court. Speaking back in August 2024, the source raged: "Huw gamed the system - and he's still doing it. He has been using the huge money given to him by the BBC to build a legal wall around himself."

‌



Meanwhile, speaking with the Mirror this week, psychotherapist Shelly Darr expressed her belief that Edwards has failed in his apparent quest to offer an explanation, not an excuse. She reflected: "The mental illness framing bothers me too. He says it can never excuse criminality, which is correct - but then does exactly that in the next breath, using it to explain why things 'fell apart.' The line between explanation and excuse is razor-thin, and I don't think he stays on the right side of it. The risk is that it asks the public to extend a sympathy that quietly minimises what real people experienced at his hands."

If you've been the victim of sexual assault, you can access help and resources via www.rapecrisis.org.uk or by calling the national telephone helpline on 0808 802 9999

You can catch Power: The Downfall of Huw Edwards at 9pm on March 24, on Channel 5.

News.title: M65 traffic LIVE: Hour-long delays after seven-vehicle crash in Lancashire
News.content:
17:29 KEY EVENT

Drivers warned of six-mile tailbacks and delays of an hour

The M65 has been closed with the entire westbound carriageway shut to traffic within the J3 Brimmicroft Interchange near Blackburn due to a collision involving at least 7 vehicles, National Highways North West has said.

The agency added that all emergency services are on scene meanwhile traffic is being diverted via the exit & entry slip roads.

Drivers have been warned to expect delays of an hour or more as tailbacks reached six miles from the site of the incident.

Meanwhile traffic monitoring service Inrix said: “M65 Westbound closed, long delays due to accident at J3 A674 Chorley Road (Blackburn West / Wheelton). Congestion to J4 (Darwen / Blackburn South). Also slow on the other side due to onlookers.

Initially reported as an obstruction, sources now confirm this to be an accident."

News.title: Doctors announce six-day NHS strike across England after Easter
News.content:
Doctors announce six-day NHS strike across England after Easter

British Medical Association announces resident doctors will go on strike just after Easter weekend for the 15th time in their pay dispute

View 3 Images Resident doctors have a mandate to strike for another six months (Image: Getty Images)

Doctors are set to go on strike for six days in what will be the 15th walkout in their pay dispute.

‌



Resident doctors will hold the strike in England from 7am on Tuesday April 7 and the action will see thousands of planned hospital appointments cancelled. The announcement of another strike by the British Medical Association comes after the Government offered doctors a 3.5% pay rise for 2026/27. It comes as the war in the Middle East is expected to cause rising inflation and on the day new data put the Retail Price Index (RPI) measure of inflation at 3.6%.

‌



Dr Jack Fletcher, chair of Resident Doctors Committee, said: “We have been negotiating in good faith for weeks to try and end the simultaneous pay and jobs crises for resident doctors. Frustratingly we had been making good progress right up until the point, in the last two weeks, when the Government began to shift the goalposts.

‌



View 3 Images Health Secretary Wes Streeting on a hospital visit (Image: Nick Wilkinson/Birmingham Live)

“As talks progressed it became clear that the money proposed for pay increases was now going to be spread over three years. This is combined with today’s pay review body (DDRB) recommendation of a 3.5% uplift pointing to yet more years in which our pay, at best, barely treads water.

“We have made abundantly clear throughout this dispute that our aim is pay restoration, and any deal that did not move us substantially in that direction was not going to fly. Dr Jack Fletcher, chair of Resident Doctors Committee, said: “We have been negotiating in good faith for weeks to try and end the simultaneous pay and jobs crises for resident doctors. Frustratingly we had been making good progress right up until the point, in the last two weeks, when the Government began to shift the goalposts.

‌



“As talks progressed it became clear that the money proposed for pay increases was now going to be spread over three years. This is combined with today’s pay review body (DDRB) recommendation of a 3.5% uplift pointing to yet more years in which our pay, at best, barely treads water.

View 3 Images Resident doctors manned picket lines during their previous strike (Image: Getty Images)

“We have made abundantly clear throughout this dispute that our aim is pay restoration, and any deal that did not move us substantially in that direction was not going to fly.”

‌



The latest round of industrial action will start just after the Easter long weekend from 7am on April 7 until 6.59am on April 13 The BMA has called for a commitment to increase pay by 26% over the next few years. The union points to pay erosion since 2008 saying real terms salaries are down a fifth since then, according to the RPI measure of inflation.

The Government’s preferred measure of inflation is the CPI - which excludes mortgage and permanent housing costs - shows average resident doctor salaries down 5% since 2008. But pay has been increasing for resident doctors in recent years, particularly since Labour came to power.

The Review Body on Doctors' and Dentists' Remuneration (DDRB) advises the Government on rates of pay for doctors and dentists. Ministers then decide whether to accept the recommendation.

‌



The Government said their offer to doctors included:

Reform of the pay structure, so resident doctors would benefit from more frequent and fairer pay rises at each stage of their training, as they reach key competencies and enhance productivity.

Combining pay structure reform with the DDRB uplift of 3.5%.

Pay rises over three years baked in, linked to the independent DDRB recommendations, as requested by the BMA.

Starting pay for new graduates entering the profession this year would have been nearly £12,000 higher than four years ago.

Up to 4,500 more specialty training places would have been created over the next 3 years, including 1,000 for this year’s applicants with the additional round of recruitment launching in April.

Reimbursement of Royal College exam fees from April this year, which resident doctors currently must pay out of pocket to progress with their training, meaning resident doctors would have been thousands of pounds better off.

Contract reform for locally employed doctors to ensure they also benefit from this new deal.

Health Secretary Wes Streeting said: “This government has pulled every lever available lever to put forward a generous package, developed in tandem with the BMA, that would have transformed the working lives and career prospects of resident doctors. I would like to thank the leaders of the BMA’s RDC for the constructive approach they have shown to the intensive talks we have had since the turn of the year. I am only sorry that this has not resulted in an agreement. The result is that resident doctors will be worse off.

‌



“My door is always open to NHS unions that want to work with the government to improve the conditions of NHS staff. The historic deal on the table demonstrates what can be achieved when we work together, rather than be trapped in a damaging cycle of industrial action. It is for that reason that I am not giving up just yet.

"I’ve gone as far as I can and the government can afford, but it is not too late for the committee to reconsider, and I urge them to do so. My focus and that of leaders across the NHS will sadly now have to turn again towards protecting patients, staff and our NHS by minimising disruption from more needless strikes."

Mr Streeting said if doctors continue to reject the deal and go on strike then they will still get the 3.5% rise but other aspects such as the 1,000 new specialty training places will no longer be affordable.

‌



A five-day walkout that resident doctors staged just before Christmas was their 14th strike since March 2023. Each of the three five-day stoppages they have held since Labour took power in July 2024 has cost the NHS an estimated £250 million.

Earlier this year it was announced that NHS staff on Agenda for Change contracts - most employees apart from senior mangers, doctors and dentists - would receive 3.3%.

Unison's head of health Helga Pile said: "Many health staff will question why colleagues working alongside them have once again got a higher pay rise for no apparent reason. The NHS acts as one team and how employees are treated should reflect this."

Article continues below

It comes after the 5.4% pay increase resident doctors were awarded for 2025/26 which the Government has described as the most generous in the public sector.

News.content: The Guthrie family donated $500,000 in February to the National Center for Missing & Exploited Children, saying they are "hoping that the attention that has been given to our mom and our family will extend to all the families like ours who are in need and need prayers and need support".

News.title: Bravery medal recipient hugged armed man to stop him bombing hospital
News.content: "It's just crazy that if I hadn't been in hospital, if I hadn't got that chest infection and been rushed to hospital, I'd have been at home, he would have gone for it and I would have been seeing it on the news."

News.title: London Marathon considers two-day event in 2027
News.content:
London Marathon organisers are considering staging next year's event across two days.

No approval has yet been given but event organisers say they are exploring ways to enable more people to take part.

It comes after a record 1.1 million people applied in the public ballot to participate in the 2026 edition - a 36% increase on the previous year.

A London Marathon Events spokesperson said: "The London Marathon is the world's most popular marathon, and we are continually exploring innovative ways to enable more people to take part, while delivering positive benefits for London.

"Together with our partners and stakeholders, we are looking at the intention for the 2027 London Marathon to take place across two days.

"Our immediate focus is on delivering an incredible 2026 London Marathon on Sunday, 26 April and ensuring every participant has an amazing experience."

Boxing.title: Usyk invited me and Anthony Joshua to Kyiv… here's what he told me about loss, AJ's return to boxing and war: CHARLOTTE DALY
Boxing.content:
Kyiv, March 21, 2026. A date I will never forget. Amid the echoes of sirens and hollowed-out streets, two heavyweight champions and rivals stand side by side in a compelling image of unity.

Oleksandr Usyk, the pride of Ukraine and former undisputed heavyweight champion, and Anthony Joshua, Britain’s two-time world champion, have come together not for a fight, but for something perhaps even more powerful: a shared purpose, a journey into the heart of a nation, and a bond forged through respect, grief and ambition.

We are here to see Usyk. No one knew that Joshua would be coming too. Every detail of the Brit's journey has been carefully planned. He flew into Poland, then drove eight hours to Lviv, the largest city in western Ukraine. My journey began with a flight to Krakow, before a two-hour bus ride to Przemysl Glowny, and a night train to Kyiv.

The cabin was tiny with four people, bunk beds on either side and bags spilling across half the space. I curled up on the top right bunk, our videographer on the bunk across from me, two strangers below.

Stepping off in Kyiv after 24 hours in transit what we find is striking. Buildings blown to pieces, craters scarring the streets and sandbags leaning against shattered windows. The centre of Kyiv, a city of three million people, is empty. My phone buzzes with missile alerts - three on the night of Usyk’s event - instructing us to take shelter, while locals moved through it as if nothing is happening.

Over the weekend, Usyk leads Joshua - who he beat in 2021 in north London and a 2022 rematch in Jeddah - through Kyiv, visiting a memorial for fallen Ukrainian soldiers, then one for British troops who had died helping Ukraine. They end at Usyk’s local church, meeting his priest and sharing a private moment of prayer, before heading to the Usyk17 Promotions Rising Stars event venue. This is what happens.

Oleksandr Usyk and Anthony Joshua sat down with Daily Mail Sport in a powerful and rare meeting of two heavyweight rivals

The two titans of boxing met in Kyiv, where Usyk showed Britain's Joshua the battered streets of his homeland

'I wanted to show Anthony my national food, my culture, Ukrainian music, everything,' Usyk, 39, tells Daily Mail Sport. 'I want him to feel my energy and understand the places that are important to me. Maidan (the main city square), for example, is now like a memorial. It’s not only about Ukrainians - it’s about all the people who help and protect Ukraine: people from the UK, Europe, the USA, Australia… the whole world.

'When we talk, I tell Anthony about the young guys, people born in the 2000s, who are going through all of this. You hear about the night bombing, the events happening here, and you realise how serious it is. He says, "Oh my God, it’s a big problem". And I say, "Yeah, brother, it’s a big problem".

'I really appreciate that he’s here. To me, he’s like a big brother. He’s not my rival, not my opponent, he’s my partner, my brother. And now he likes Ukrainian food too, especially borscht.'

The word 'brother' hangs over everything that follows. Because to understand what is happening between Usyk and Joshua, you have to understand Joshua’s recent journey.
